quotes.verbatim_quote:
In this lesson we have 4 examples of Symetric Cycles. It isn't necessary to write
them in full detail. Only the first chord will be diagrammed and those to follow, as you
should know, are a minor third higher.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the core concept of the lesson — symmetric cycles built by repeating a chord shape in ascending minor thirds.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
In exercise 32 the fourth finger moves from the 2nd to the 3rd string as diagrammed.

Simply follow the same fingering as you progress up the finger board in minor thirds.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Gives a specific left-hand fingering instruction — the fourth finger string transfer.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
‘These exercises can be extended still higher if you have a Cutaway guitar. If not, carry
them as high as your guitar will allow.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Sets a practical rule for range limits based on instrument type.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Any chord can be played in a Symetric Cycle. Take any of your favorite chords, start
them in a low position on your guitar and move them up in minor thirds or start them in
a high position and move them down in minor thirds.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Generalizes the technique beyond the four written exercises.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Play the exercises below backwards, starting with T1 then 4, 3, 2 and back to the 1.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Practice directive — playing the cycle in reverse order to train descending motion.
